response: The sun slanted through the smudged front window of Maple Street’s only used bookstore, gilding the stack of tattered children’s picture books I’d dug out from a back closet that morning. Tucked between the pages of a water-stained copy of *Where the Wild Things Are* was a crumpled receipt dated 1998, scrawled on the back in purple crayon: “This is for Lila, who roars louder than Max ever could.” I’d been eight when my dad bought that book for me, the same year we lost our apartment and I left half my toy box and favorite stories on the curb in a panic. A gray-haired man hovering by the poetry shelf cleared his throat, and when I turned he held up a matching water-stained copy of *Charlotte’s Web*, the same one I’d remembered leaving beside the Wild Things book that same day. He’d spent three years scouring every used bookstore within 50 miles, he said, after he’d saved enough to rent a house with a bookshelf for all the things we’d left behind. I left the store that afternoon with both books tucked under my arm, and for the first time since I was eight, I got to roar for Max all over again at the dinner table we shared that night.
